subject: Infectious Disease
question: A 70-year-old with UTI presents with fever 39°C, HR 115, RR 28, BP 85/50, lactate 4.2 mmol/L. What is the MOST time-sensitive intervention?

options.text: IV antibiotics within 1 hour + IV fluids 30mL/kg
options.explanation: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: antibiotics within 1 hour (each hour delay increases mortality 7-8%). IV crystalloids 30mL/kg for hypotension. Measure lactate, blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics. Vasopressors if refractory hypotension.

options.text: Obtain CT scan to find source
options.explanation: Imaging should NOT delay antibiotics. Source identification is important but secondary to early antibiotics and fluids. Can image after stabilization.
Vasopressors indicated if hypotension persists AFTER fluid resuscitation (30mL/kg). Not first-line before fluid challenge. Norepinephrine is first-choice vasopressor.

options.text: Wait for culture results before antibiotics
options.explanation: NEVER delay antibiotics awaiting cultures. Obtain cultures THEN start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately. De-escalate when sensitivities return.

options.text: Transfer to ICU after stabilization on floor
options.explanation: Septic shock requires ICU level care. Transfer should be expedited, not delayed for floor stabilization. Early ICU admission improves outcomes.

educationalObjective: Implement Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundle: blood cultures, lactate, antibiotics within 1 hour, IV fluids 30mL/kg, vasopressors if needed.
keyTakeaway: Sepsis: Antibiotics within 1 hour (do NOT delay). Fluids 30mL/kg. Cultures first. Lactate q2-4h to monitor clearance. Norepinephrine if refractory hypotension.
learning_objective: Master this concept in Infectious Disease
